entry.title: [Performance History] it cannot show ranking label instead of number
entry.content:
Hi there,

Short Description / Summary:

After upgrading to the latest version of the Employee Profile (People Profile), the Performance History block only displays numeric scores (e.g., "5") instead of rating labels (e.g., "Exceeds Expectations"). We request SAP to restore the option to display text-based rating labels to improve user experience.

Business Case / Description:

Following our recent upgrade to the latest version of the Employee Profile, we noticed a significant regression in the Performance History section.

  • Previous Behavior (Legacy Version): The system allowed us to configure and display text-based rating labels(e.g., "Exceeds Expectations") rather than just a raw number.

  • Current Behavior (Latest Version): The block now strictly displays numeric ratings (e.g., "5").

Why this is an issue: Displaying only numbers is highly counterintuitive and creates an unfriendly user experience for both employees and managers. Users often do not remember what each number stands for, forcing them to manually look up the rating scale mapping. This change has led to unnecessary confusion and extra administrative inquiries for our HR team.

Suggested Solution:

We kindly request SAP to bring back the parity from the previous version. Please provide a configuration option or enhancement in the latest Employee Profile that allows organizations to choose whether to display numeric scores, text rating labels, or both in the Performance History block.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 05 - Portable functions
Enhance Clock In / Clock Out UX with a One-Click Direct Home Page Widget
entry.content:
Hi there,

Short Description / Summary:

The current Clock In / Clock Out (CICO) functionality in SuccessFactors requires too many clicks and navigation steps, making it unintuitive for daily use. We propose a dedicated, static Home Page widget/card that allows employees to clock in, clock out, or go on break with a single click, showing their live status at a glance.

Business Case / Description:

Clocking in and out is a daily, high-frequency action for standard employees. However, the current SuccessFactors UI requires users to navigate through multiple menus or handle pop-up windows just to register a timestamp.

This lack of intuitiveness often leads to:

  • Forgotten punches: Employees forget to clock in/out because the status isn't prominently visible.

  • Wasted time: Navigating the system daily for a simple action creates cumulative productivity loss.

  • High error rates: Users occasionally select the wrong time event type due to non-intuitive dropdown designs.

Proposed Intuitive UI Layout (Concept):

We request SAP to design a streamlined, direct-action card on the Reimagined Home Page that resembles the following layout:

Key Features of this Proposed UI:

  1. Dynamic Status Indicator: Clearly displays whether the user is currently "Clocked In", "Clocked Out", or "On Break" using color-coded text/icons.

  2. One-Click Execution: Clicking "Clock In" instantly registers the timestamp based on local system time withoutopening additional confirmation pop-ups.

  3. Smart Button Toggling: Buttons automatically grey out or activate based on the current state (e.g., if already clocked in, the "Clock In" button becomes unclickable).

Suggested Solution:

Provide a dedicated, high-visibility standard card for the SuccessFactors Home Page specifically for Time Tracking / CICO users, minimizing administrative overhead and maximizing employee adoption.

Node.js environment

Fiori tools (@sap/fiori)

Expected Behavior

Deployment configuration should be added successfully and allow deployment to ABAP system.

Additional Notes

entry.title: Re: Integration suite API that retrieves the message size
entry.content:
The MessageProcessingLogs API is the correct monitoring API, but it does not provide payload size natively. Therefore, implementing a Groovy step that calculates the payload size and stores it as a Custom Header Property is the recommended and commonly used approach for making payload size available through the MPL OData API.

SAP help document: Get Custom Header Properties | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: BAdI for Filter in Retail Fiori app Order Products
entry.content:
The Retail Fiori App Order Products (F0752) can be extended with custom filters using BAdI's BADI_RTST_OP_FILTER and BADI_RTST_OP_FILTERVALUE. This works fine; filters and their values appear in the app. When selecting a filter value however, the app breaks with message "Property x not found in type StoreProduct" because the filter field is not available in the metadata of the service. The app is based on SEGW service RETAILSTORE_ORDER_PRODUCT.  

The documentation off these BAdI's gives the following information:
For each new filter, a new corresponding property has to be created in the entity StoreProduct of the OData Service RETAILSTORE_ORDER_PRODUCT_SRV. The name of the property has to correspond to the ID of the filter so that the UI can map the filter value to the respective property. A new field also has to be created in the structure RTST_OP_S_STORE_PRODUCT_INCL that must be mapped to the new property in the entity.

There seems to be no BAdI in the SEGW service itself to add the property so how can the property be added in a clean way? Creating a custom service to redefine the standard service appears to be the only way to manage custom filters. Or am I overlooking an option?

entry.title: Can Tax Calculation in Expense Reports be restricted to specific Expense Types?
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are currently investigating the Expense Report functionality in SAP Business ByDesign.

We found that VAT/Tax-related processing in Expense Reports appears to be available through the Tax Activation - Generic Country/Region Version configuration activity ("Activate Tax Calculation").

Our customer has the following requirement:

  • Only certain expense types are eligible for VAT claims.
  • Other expense types should not be subject to VAT processing.
  • Therefore, they would like to know whether tax-related processing (such as Tax Code selection or tax calculation) can be enabled only for specific Expense Types.

Example:

  • Taxi Expense → VAT applicable
  • Hotel Expense → VAT applicable
  • Employee Allowance → VAT not applicable
  • Mileage Claim → VAT not applicable

We would like to understand:

  1. After Tax Calculation is activated for Expense Reports, can Tax Code selection be restricted to specific Expense Types only?
  2. Is it possible to define certain Expense Types as VAT-relevant and others as non-VAT-relevant within standard ByDesign functionality?
  3. If this is not supported, what is the recommended standard approach to handle situations where only some employee expenses are eligible for VAT claims?
  4. Are there any known limitations or best practices regarding Tax Activation in Expense Reports?

We have reviewed KBA 2546221 regarding Tax Activation for Expense Reports, but could not find information about Expense-Type-specific control. (https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2917069)

Any experience or recommendations would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Supplier Down payment adjustments and tracking
entry.content:
Hi Vagula,

Thank you for your follow-up question. Because the actual clearing happens in the FI module, you need to look at the financial documents to get this granular view. Here is how you can track this using the standard apps, along with an option to build a custom report:

  1. Manage Supplier Line Items - This is the most precise standard app for tracking these adjustments. When a specific down payment is cleared against a specific supplier invoice, the system links both documents together using a Clearing Journal Entry.
  • Use the following columns in your layout: Clearing Journal Entry, Journal Entry (Document Number), Posting Date, Clearing Date, Special G/L Indicator, Purchasing Document, and Reference (this is where your proforma invoice numbers are typically stored).
  • How to read it: Group or sort the list by the Clearing Journal Entry. Under each unique clearing number, you will see exactly which Down Payment document (e.g., Special G/L 'A') and which final Supplier Invoice(s) were matched and adjusted against each other, along with their exact dates.
  1. Building a Custom Report (Custom Analytical Queries) If your team finds the standard FI app too manual and requires a dedicated, single-screen dashboard showing exactly: Proforma Date -> Advance Amount -> Final Invoice Date -> Adjusted Amount, you will need to build a custom report.

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, you can achieve this using standard Key User Extensibility:

  • Your analytics expert can use the Custom Analytical Queries app.
  • They can build a query on top of standard released CDS views (such as I_SupplierLineItem or I_JournalEntryItem). If not sufficient, you need to build your custom SDC view and combine the necessary standard ones.
  • The logic: The query needs to be designed to group or join the logistical parameters (PO number, supplier Invoice) and the financial documents (Advance Payments and Supplier Invoices) using the Clearing Journal Entry (ClearingAccountingDocument) as the linking key. Note – In the reference document on the JE item level of the invoice, the supplier Invoice number is stored. You can use the PO number/Item to link these documents to build a complex report.

Hope this helps.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Secondary Cost G/Ls Not Visible in Project Costs App
entry.content:
Good day @rohini_dutta21 

Thank you for your question.

Please be aware that the project cost does not simply display every posting that is visible in Display Line Items. The G/L account must be correctly assigned in Financial Statement Version YPS2, and the related financial statement item must be assigned to the correct semantic tag, such as ACT_COST. See KBA 3453098 for more details.

To put that in simple terms:

  • If a secondary cost G/L account is posted to the project but not mapped correctly in YPS2, it may not appear in the Project Costs app.
  • Display Line Items shows journal entry line items, so it can show more details than the analytical project cost app.
  • It is not correct to say that YPS2 must only contain Primary Cost and Revenue G/L accounts.
  • Secondary cost G/L accounts can be included if they are relevant for project actual cost reporting and are assigned under the correct YPS2 node.
  • Do not add secondary cost accounts randomly. Only add the relevant secondary cost elements that should be treated as project costs in the report.
  • After changing YPS2, run Replicate Runtime Hierarchy so that the report can consume the updated hierarchy.

A useful point is that in Public Cloud, secondary cost elements are also G/L accounts with account type Secondary Cost, used for internal value flows such as allocations.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/13b27f3ce6d44b6888c53244eaa4581d.html?locale=en-US

In summary:

The behaviour you see is standard. The Project Costs app depends on the reporting structure and semantic tag assignment in YPS2, while Display Line Items shows the journal entry postings directly. If secondary cost G/L accounts should be included in project cost reporting, they must be assigned to the correct node in YPS2, and the related financial statement item must be assigned to ACT_COST. After the change, run Replicate Runtime Hierarchy and test the report again.  

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Summarize values for total amount of rows
entry.content: Hi Jessica, you get an absolutely expected result for the formula you are performing. Measure "Count" = count of your records (9061). If you need a total amount of hours you need to use it as a main measure, and not count. You can assign it to be a measure in the query (semantic type measure instead of dimension).

entry.title: Best Practice for Month-End Allocation of a Single Material into Two Sales Order Items
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud and looking for a fit-to-standard approach.

Business process:

  • During the month, customers make multiple advance payments related to a single material.
  • According to local requirements, a consolidated tax invoice is issued based on the received advances.
  • At that stage, only one material is known and final quantities and prices are not yet available.
  • There is no outbound delivery process involved.
  • At month end, business users manually determine the final quantities and prices.
  • For reporting purposes, the original material must be split into two separate items with different account assignment groups and revenue accounts.
  • Users want to manually maintain and control quantities and values for these two items.
  • The allocation between the two items is determined manually by business users at month end and may vary from month to month.

Currently, we are considering creating a Sales Order at the beginning of the month and manually updating it at month end with the final quantities and values before billing.

However, this creates double maintenance of the Sales Order.

Since users want to manually control the final quantities and values, the Sales Order seems to be the most appropriate document for maintaining this information.

Has anyone implemented a similar process?

Is there a standard SAP approach that avoids repeated Sales Order maintenance while still allowing:

  • advance payment processing,
  • consolidated tax invoice processing,
  • manual month-end quantity/value entry,
  • allocate a single material into two separate items,
  • and final invoicing?

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: India GST 5% tax codes are missing in S/4HANA Cloud P...
entry.content:
Dear Customer, 

India is enabled for TDT (Time-Dependent Tax) Solution. Time Dependent Taxes (TDT) enables you to create tax codes, edit Tax Code, change tax rates with validity periods to your existing tax codes in the event of a legal change.

Please follow the resolution steps provided in ##0003763311 - GST Tax Codes for India are Missing From the Standard Tax Code List.

You are able to create your own tax codes according to the legal changes. 

For detailed steps on how to create tax code, you can refer to the KBA "3009061 - How to create tax code in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition" 

Regards, 

Arjun

entry.title: Re: how to custom agent for sales price approval
entry.content: Hi Christ, Thank you for your proposal. As I understand it, you are suggesting we use the standard fields of the price workflow and price teams. However, the price workflow currently can only be determined by Sales Org, Distribution Channel, Division, and Condition Type. We previously proposed routing the criteria by Distribution Channel, but this was rejected by the client. Furthermore, these three factors are heavily linked to Sales Org (SD) and have a massive impact. Since the sales price team can also only be determined by Condition Type, using the standard functionality will not help us split the price approval workflow. Regarding custom responsibility management, SAP provides two BAdIs. The first is RSM_BADI_CONTEXT_ATTRIB_CALC, which is used to modify/get the value of custom fields to act as a custom responsibility definition. Therefore, theoretically, I believe it can be used as a solution for Scenario 1 (retrieving the custom field value and comparing it with the team's price channel value to trigger the corresponding team). In fact, during testing within the BAdI code, when we hardcoded a specific value (e.g., 101), only team members assigned to value 101 received the approval notification. Because of this, I believe the team approval mechanism is also driven by more granular controls at the team level, rather than strictly following the Sales Order Approver Level 1 at the price workflow level. For Scenario 2, I noticed SAP provides the BAdI RSM_BADI_RESPONSIBILITY_RULE. In my opinion, if we can fetch the CreatedByUser of the condition record, we can create a custom mapping table between creators and approvers to activate the respective approver.

entry.title: Re: Purchasing Document and Purchasing Document Item Not...
entry.content:
Dear @vivekvyas,

Thank you for raising this query.

Yes, this is standard and expected system behavior. In the Manage Supplier Line Items app, the Purchasing Document and Purchasing Document Item columns are available in the report layout; however, they will typically appear blank or show a default value of "00000" for most supplier invoice line items. This is by design and is documented in the SAP Help:
[Manage Supplier Line Items: FAQ]

The reason for this behavior is rooted in how purchase orders and supplier invoices are structured:
Multiple purchase orders can be consolidated into a single invoice. Since there is generally exactly one line item per invoice, the system is unable to establish a unique, unambiguous link back to a specific purchase order at the line item level — and therefore cannot display the Purchasing Document details in this context.

There is no configuration or setting that will enable these values to be populated for standard invoice line items, as this is a fundamental system design behavior and not a gap or defect. If you need to identify document relationships, you can consider the following alternatives:

  • Add the "Original Reference Document" and "Reference Document Type" columns to the table in the Manage Supplier Line Items app to identify the supplier invoice number.
  • Use the Manage Journal Entries app and navigate to the Related Documents tab to get a complete overview of all documents related to a specific line item.

A valid question is why the Purchasing Document and Purchasing Document Item columns exist in the app if they are generally not populated. The reason is that there are exceptional use cases where these fields can be filled. A key example is the Supplier Down Payment Requests. When a down payment request is created for a supplier, the fields Purchasing Document and Purchasing Document Item can be explicitly maintained. In such cases, the values will be displayed correctly in these columns.

Best regards
Steffen

entry.title: Live session: SAP EHS Risk Assessment in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud | June 10, 2026 | 15:00 CET
entry.content:
Most companies don't have a safety problem. They have a visibility problem.

Risks tracked in spreadsheets. Safety data scattered across emails and disconnected tools. Hazard assessments that are outdated, buried, or missing entirely. And when something finally goes wrong, the uncomfortable truth often surfaces: the risk was known. It just wasn't visible in time to act on it.

This is exactly what SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud for EHS solves.

On June 10 at 15:00 CET, LeverX and SAP are hosting a free one-hour live session on EHS Risk Assessment with a hands-on demo showing how structured safety processes in SAP S/4HANA can transform reactive firefighting into genuine preventive control.

What you'll see

In 60 minutes, you'll discover how to identify and assess risks before they turn into incidents, structure safety processes consistently across your organization, and ensure compliance through clear, auditable workflows. The session includes a live demo covering chemical approval workflows, Operational Risk Assessment, Job Hazard Analysis, safety instructions for equipment and substances, and workplace safety analytics; all within standard SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud functionality

Who should attend

This session is designed for EHS and HSE professionals seeking a structured approach beyond spreadsheets, risk and compliance managers, operations and plant managers who need real-time visibility, sustainability and ESG leaders, SAP and IT architects, and quality or process improvement managers embedding safety into operations.

Your speakers

Vladimir Voronov, SAP EHS & Sustainability Delivery Lead at LeverX, and Leyi Liu, Product Marketing Manager at SAP, will guide you through the session and take your questions live.

Event details

Date: June 10, 2026 Time: 15:00 CET Duration: 1 hour Language: English Registration: Risk Assessment & Workplace Safety in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

entry.title: Asset Revaluation - SAP Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I'm working with Asset Accounting in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud for Portugal and I have a question regarding asset revaluation.

In the Portuguese localization, when performing an asset revaluation, I would like to understand the correct accounting approach:

  • Is it possible to post the revaluation directly to account 5891 (Excedentes de Revalorização), or should a clearing account be used as an intermediate step before transferring to equity? If a clearing account is used, how and when does the balance get transferred to the final equity account — is this automatic or manual?
  • Does SAP standard use a dedicated clearing account during the revaluation process in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, or does it post directly to equity?
  • What is the expected behaviour regarding depreciation after revaluation? Is depreciation recalculated automatically based on the revalued asset base, and does the system automatically handle the gradual realisation of the revaluation surplus from 5891 to Retained Earnings?
  • How are deferred taxes handled in this scenario? Are they automatically calculated and posted by the system, or do they require manual configuration and posting?

I would appreciate any clarification on the standard best practice for Portugal in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: issue in useful life calculation and the depreciation...
entry.content:
Hi,

has the asset been created by a Lease Contract? If so, then you have to check the (asset) values from the Lease Contract, as the complete depreciation is calculated based on your lease data and parameters. 

In the documentation for Base Method: Depreciation Calculation Methods you find the following statement:

Depreciation from real estate leasing: When using this method, the depreciation is determined by the contract values for the lease and not by Asset Accounting.

Kind Regards,
Markus

entry.title: Re: issue in useful life calculation and the depreciation...
entry.content:
Hi Gomathi,

Based on standard SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition behavior, the issue where the depreciation start date shifts to April 1st and the useful life is reduced to 35 months is typically caused by the interaction between the Period Control Method, the Asset Value Date of your June posting, and the fixed lease terms of ROU assets.

Here is an objective breakdown of the system logic causing this:

1. Period Control Method (PCM) Overriding Manual Dates

In SAP Asset Accounting, the system does not strictly lock a manually entered 'Depreciation Start Date' when subsequent transactions occur. The actual start period is dynamically derived from the Asset Value Date of the acquisition posting, adjusted by the Period Control Method assigned to your Depreciation Key.

  • If your Depreciation Key uses a Period Control for 'Acquisitions' set to a logic like '11' (Next month), an asset with an asset value date of March 1st will automatically begin depreciating on April 1st.

  • The late acquisition posting on June 1st triggered a recalculation. During this recalculation, the system reapplied the Period Control rules, overriding your manual March 1st entry.

2. Automatic Reduction of Useful Life (36 to 35 months)

For Right-of-Use (ROU) assets, the end of the useful life is strictly tied to the fixed end date of the lease contract. Because the system's Period Control shifted the start of depreciation forward by one month (to April 1st), the remaining duration until the fixed lease end date shrank to exactly 35 months. The system automatically reduces the useful life to align the depreciation schedule with the immutable contract expiration date.

Steps to Verify in the System:

  1. Check the Depreciation Key: Open the app Depreciation Key: Display Details. Verify the Period Control Method assigned to the 'Acquisition' transaction type. If depreciation must start in March, the system requires a key with a Period Control such as '01' (Pro rata at period start date).

  2. Verify the Asset Value Date: Check the financial document posted on June 1st. Confirm whether the 'Asset Value Date' was explicitly set to March 1st at the time of posting.

Questions for Clarification:

To provide a definitive solution, please clarify the following:

  1. What is the exact Depreciation Key assigned to this asset, and what is its specific Period Control Method for acquisitions?

  2. Are you managing this ROU asset through the standard SAP RE-FX (Contract and Lease Management) integration, or was this a manual FI-AA acquisition posting?

  3. What exact 'Asset Value Date' was entered on the journal entry posted on June 1st?

For further reference on standard date logic, please review the official documentation: Dates in Asset Accounting

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: EDI in the age of agentic AI, the structural mismatch nobody is naming
entry.content:
Somewhere in most people’s houses, there is a road atlas. Beautifully printed, comprehensive, the place you were looking for always on the join between two pages or on the fold where the binding had eaten the postcode. Then the internet arrived and you printed out a route from a website, which was a digital map in name and a dead letter on the passenger seat. The thing that actually changed driving was location services. The moment the map knew where you were and where the closures were, it stopped being an artefact and became a network you could query. Then Waze went further: routing you using information contributed by every other driver on the road. A car on the hard shoulder. A speed camera. A tailback the official feed would not catch for twenty minutes. The map was no longer a thing you read. It was a network where every participant made the whole more intelligent for everyone else.

So, time to drag this back to my actual day job, which is business networks and S/4HANA. Every S/4 programme has to answer a deceptively simple question: how will this enterprise communicate with the outside world once the transformation is done? The old plumbing breaks. IDocs are restructured or retired, partner mappings need re-testing, and on-premises middleware that has quietly carried the business for fifteen years, invisible until the moment it failed, cannot keep up. The bill comes due. But the real choice is not how to fix the plumbing. It is whether the connectivity layer should remain a way of moving documents between two known parties, or become a queryable network that an autonomous agent can reason over. The answer the market is offering, namely modernise the EDI, move it to the cloud, add a bit of AI, is the corporate equivalent of printing out a route from the website. Technically more modern. Functionally the same piece of paper.

Give cloud EDI its due first. Moving an on-premises EDI estate into a managed cloud genuinely helps: better resilience, faster partner onboarding, centralised monitoring, and no more single-point-of-failure when your one EDI specialist retires. I am not arguing against any of it. I am arguing that none of it changes what the architecture is.

Cloud EDI is still EDI. It is still bilateral. It is still static. It produces a dead document, accurate at the moment of transmission, useless the moment anything changes, and it has nothing useful to offer an autonomous agent. Agents do not just process documents after the fact. They need to interrogate live, networked data, who has capacity now, at what price, under what constraints, and EDI has no way of exposing that.

This is the part that needs spelling out. Consider a worked example. A buyer agent at a Tier 1 manufacturer receives an order for three thousand units of a sub-assembly. To place that order well, the agent needs to know which suppliers across the network have capacity, at what price, with what lead time, tariff exposure, and carbon intensity. It needs to consider whether splitting the order across two suppliers reduces risk enough to justify the higher unit price, and whether either supplier is constrained by a Tier 2 component that has gone short this week. I have sat through several of those demos this year. Not one of them could have been accomplished over a one-to-one document pipe. Not one. The agent needs a network where it can ask a question and get an answer that reflects the current state of that network.

The architectural requirement is the same whatever you call it: networked, queryable, shared state across thousands of partners, with event-level visibility and the means to act. SAP Business Network is the specific instantiation of this within the S/4HANA ecosystem. The label matters less than the property. EDI was engineered in the 1970s to do the opposite of all of it.

Watch the market on this one. Read any vendor argument for "modernising your EDI to the cloud" carefully and you will find them describing a system that decouples mapping from the ERP, holds partner profiles centrally, and offers a real-time cockpit across thousands of partners. That is not modernised EDI. That is a network with a different name on the door. The market is quietly building the right thing while loudly selling the wrong word for it.

Two numbers tell you the shape of it. Interos, in its 6 Supply Chain Risks for 2026 report, finds that 90% of organisations remain blind to sub-tier disruptions for up to 48 hours. APQC's 2026 Supply Chain Priorities and Challenges: Cross-Industry Report finds that only one in three organisations have a strategy this year to increase visibility into lower-tier suppliers. Two-thirds are not even planning to do anything about it. One-to-one connections cannot reach those lower tiers. They cannot reach an agentic workflow either.

You already know I work in this space. The companies already on a network are building compound advantage in supplier visibility, agentic readiness, and network intelligence that a one-to-one pipe cannot replicate after the fact.

This should not be a Phase 2 conversation. It should not be kicked to 2028. It should not be left for the next team to discover. By then the programme team has moved on. The decision is business as usual. Nobody around the table remembers it was once a choice. A one-to-one pipe cannot reroute when a supplier fails, recompute when a tariff lands, or answer when an agent asks it a question. A network can do all three. The companies that defer will spend the next three years falling behind quietly, and in an agentic world three years is no longer a planning horizon. It is a generation. Kicking this to 2028 is not a deferral. It is a decision to fall behind, taken by people who will not be there to see it land.

The road atlas works. So does the printed page from the route planner. Neither of them is going to tell you about the lorry on the hard shoulder at the next junction, or that a faster route has just opened up that the rest of your competitors are already taking.

entry.title: SAPUI5 CRUD Without OData — A Complete Guide Using Node.js, Express, and MySQL
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Introduction

When building applications with SAPUI5, we usually reach for standard SAP backends, Cloud Application Programming Model (CAPM) entities, or ABAP-backed OData services. However, in many modern agile architectures, developers need the freedom to build standalone, lightweight business utilities using open-source technologies.

In this blog post, we will explore how to build a simple Employee Management Application completely from scratch using:

  • SAPUI5 for a clean, responsive freestyle frontend layout
  • Node.js & Express.js for handling backend routing and REST endpoints
  • MySQL Workbench for reliable database storage
  • jQuery AJAX & Fetch APIs to connect the layers smoothly without standard OData overhead

This blog focuses on performing full CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) using SAPUI5, Node.js, and MySQL Workbench.

Architecture & Folder Structure Overview

To keep the code clean and well-organized, our workspace is divided into two separate root modules: backend and frontend.

BLOG/
├── backend/                     ← Node.js (Express) server
│   ├── controller/              ← Core logic functions
│   │   └── employeeController.js
│   ├── repos/                   ← Database connection setup
│   │   └── dbconnector.js
│   ├── routes/                  ← Express request endpoints
│   │   └── employeeRoutes.js
│   ├── app.js                   ← Application entry point
│   └── package.json
└── frontend/                    ← SAPUI5 application
    └── webapp/
        ├── controller/
        │   ├── App.controller.js
        │   └── View1.controller.js   ← UI control logic
        ├── view/
        │   ├── App.view.xml
        │   └── View1.view.xml        ← Split-screen layout view
        ├── Component.js
        └── manifest.json             ← Routing configuration

Step 1: Setting Up the MySQL Database

We will start by creating a schema and a data table inside MySQL Workbench to store employee records. Open an SQL tab and run the following script:

CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS employeedb;
USE employeedb;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS employees (
    emp_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    emp_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    designation VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    department VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

Figure 1: employeedb schema created in MySQL Workbench

Step 2: Backend — Setting Up the Service

Go to your backend/ folder to set up dependencies, create the database connection, map routes, and start the web server.

1. Dependencies Setup (package.json)

Run the following commands in your terminal to create your package.json and install the required packages:

npm init -y
npm install express mysql2 cors

  • npm init -y: Instantly creates the mandatory package.json file. This file tracks your project name, version, and installed package dependencies.

  • npm install express mysql2 cors: Downloads the core engine dependencies into your node_modules folder so your JavaScript server can function:

    • express: Provides the HTTP web framework tools to build your custom REST API routes.

    • mysql2: Connects Node.js to your MySQL Workbench database. Acts as the database connector bridge allowing Node.js to execute standard SQL statements directly inside MySQL Workbench.

    • cors: Allows your SAPUI5 frontend (port 8080) to communicate with your Node.js server (port 3000) without browser security errors.

Your package.json dependencies section should look like this: 

"dependencies": {
  "cors": "^2.8.6",
  "express": "^5.2.1",
  "mysql2": "^3.22.4"
}
2. Database Connection (repos/dbconnector.js)

This file connects Node.js to your local MySQL server:

const mysql = require('mysql2');

const db = mysql.createConnection({
    host: "localhost",
    user: 'root',
    password: 'Password@123', // Your MySQL Workbench password
    database: "employeedb"
});

db.connect((err) => {
    if (err) {
        console.error('Database connection failed:', err);
        return;
    }
    console.log('Connected to employeedb successfully.');
});

module.exports = db;
3. Express Routes (routes/employeeRoutes.js)

This file maps incoming HTTP requests to the correct controller functions:

const express = require('express');
const router = express.Router();
const empController = require('../controller/employeeController');

router.get('/employees', empController.getAllEmployees);
router.post('/employees', empController.createEmployee);
router.put('/employees/:id', empController.updateEmployee);
router.delete('/employees/:id', empController.deleteEmployee);

module.exports = router;
4. Main Server File (app.js)

This file starts the server on port 3000. We use cors so that the SAPUI5 frontend (running on port 8080) can send requests to the Express backend safely:

const express = require('express');
const cors = require('cors');
const app = express();
const port = 3000;

// Allow cross-origin script requests from UI5 server
app.use(cors());

// Automatically parse text payloads into JSON objects
app.use(express.json());

// Routes
app.use('/api', require('./routes/employeeRoutes')); // Base routing path prefix

app.listen(port, () => {
  console.log(`Backend server running on http://localhost:${port}`);
});

Step 3: Backend — Writing the CRUD Controller

Inside controller/employeeController.js, we write SQL queries using ? placeholders to safely interact with the database.

const db = require('../repos/dbconnector');

// CREATE
function createEmployee(req, res){

    const { emp_name, email, designation, department } = req.body;

    const sql = `INSERT INTO employees (emp_name, email, designation, department) VALUES (?,?,?,?)`;

    db.query(sql, [emp_name, email, designation, department], (err, result)=>{
        if(err){
            return res.status(500).json({ success: false, error: err.message });
        }
        res.status(201).json({ success: true, message: "Employee Added", emp_id: result.insertId });
    })
}

// READ ALL
function getAllEmployees(req, res){
    const sql = `SELECT * FROM employees`;
    db.query(sql, (err, results)=>{
        if(err) res.status(500).json({ success: false, error: err.message });
        res.json(results);
    });
};

// UPDATE
function updateEmployee(req, res){
    const { id } = req.params;
    const { emp_name, email, designation, department } = req.body;

    const sql = `UPDATE employees SET emp_name=?, email=?, designation=?, department=? WHERE emp_id=?`;
    db.query(sql, [emp_name, email, designation, department, id], (err,result)=>{
        if(err) return res.status(500).json({ success: false, error: err.message });
        res.json({ success: true, message: "Employee profile updated successfully."})
    });
};

// DELETE
function deleteEmployee(req,res){
    const { id } = req.params;
    const sql = `DELETE FROM employees WHERE emp_id = ?`;
    db.query(sql, [id], (err, result)=>{
        if(err) return res.status(500).json({ success: false, message: err.message });
        res.json({ success: true, message: "Employee record removed."})
    })
}

module.exports = { createEmployee, getAllEmployees, updateEmployee, deleteEmployee};



Figure 2: Sample employee records inserted in the database

Step 4: Frontend — Building the UI Split-View Layout

Our view uses an HBox split layout — a table on the left showing the employee list (60% width), and an editable form on the right for managing employee details (40% width). 

<mvc:View xmlns:form="sap.ui.layout.form" controllerName="frontend.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
    <HBox fitContainer="true" renderType="Bare">
        <VBox width="60%" class="sapUiSmallMargin">
            <Table id="empTable" items="{empModel>/}" mode="SingleSelectMaster" selectionChange="onEmployeeSelect">
                <headerToolbar>
                    <OverflowToolbar>
                        <Title text="Active Team Members" level="H2"/>
                        <ToolbarSpacer/>
                        <!-- <Button icon="sap-icon://refresh" press="loadEmployees"/> -->
                    </OverflowToolbar>
                </headerToolbar>

                <columns>
                    <Column><Text text="ID"></Text></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Name"></Text></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Role"></Text></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Department"></Text></Column>
                </columns>
                <items>
                    <ColumnListItem type="Active">
                        <Text text="{empModel>emp_id}"></Text>
                        <Text text="{empModel>emp_name}"></Text>
                        <Text text="{empModel>designation}"></Text>
                        <Text text="{empModel>department}"></Text>  
                    </ColumnListItem>
                </items>
            </Table>
        </VBox>

        <VBox width="40%" class="sapUiSmallMargin">
            <form:SimpleForm title="Manage Profile" layout="ResponsiveGridLayout">
                <Label text="Name" required="true"></Label>
                <Input value="{formModel>/emp_name}" placeholder="Enter employee name"></Input>

                <Label text="Email" required="true"></Label>
                <Input value="{formModel>/email}" placeholder="Enter employee email"></Input>

                <Label text="Designation" required="true"></Label>
                <Input value="{formModel>/designation}" placeholder="Enter employee role"></Input>

                <Label text="Department"/>
                <Select selectedKey="{formModel>/department}">
                    <core:Item key="" text="-- Select Department --"/>
                    <core:Item key="Management" text="Management"/>
                    <core:Item key="Engineering" text="Engineering"/>
                    <core:Item key="Human Resources" text="Human Resources"/>
                    <core:Item key="Finance" text="Finance" />
                    <core:Item key="Marketing" text="Marketing" />
                </Select>
            </form:SimpleForm>

            <HBox justifyContent="Center" class="sapUiTinyMarginTop">
                <Button text="Clear" press="onClearForm" class="sapUiTinyMarginEnd"/>
                <Button text="Delete" type="Reject" press="onDeleteEmployee" class="sapUiTinyMarginEnd"></Button>
                <Button text="Save" type="Emphasized" press="onSaveEmployee" class="sapUiTinyMarginEnd"></Button>
            </HBox>
        </VBox>
    </HBox>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

Figure 3: Initial application view with empty employee table and department dropdown

Step 5: Frontend — Handling CRUD Operations in the Controller

In this freestyle setup, we work directly with raw JSON arrays instead of OData structures. The onSaveEmployee function checks whether to send a POST (create) or PUT (update) request based on whether an employee ID already exists:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/m/MessageBox"
], (Controller, JSONModel, MessageToast, MessageBox) => {
    "use strict";

    const BASE_URL = "http://localhost:3000/api/employees";

    return Controller.extend("frontend.controller.View1", {
        onInit() {
            this.getView().setModel(new JSONModel([]), "empModel");
            this.getView().setModel(new JSONModel({}), "formModel");
            this._loadEmployees();
        },
        _loadEmployees: function(){
            fetch(BASE_URL)
            .then(res => res.json())
            .then(data => {
                this.getView().getModel("empModel").setData(data);
            })
            .catch(err =>{
                MessageToast.show("Error fetching server data.");
            })
        },
        onEmployeeSelect: function (oEvent) {
            const oCtx = oEvent.getParameter("listItem").getBindingContext("empModel");
            // Perform a deep clone to safely isolate edit state from the live table array
            const oSelectedData = Object.assign({}, oCtx.getObject());
            this.getView().getModel("formModel").setData(oSelectedData);
        },
        // CREATE & UPDATE (Merged using $.ajax)
        onSaveEmployee: function () {
            const oData = this.getView().getModel("formModel").getData();

            if (!oData.emp_name || !oData.email || !oData.department) {
                return MessageToast.show("Name, Email, and Department are mandatory.");
            }

            const isUpdate = !!oData.emp_id;
            
            $.ajax({
                url: isUpdate ? `${BASE_URL}/${oData.emp_id}` : BASE_URL,
                method: isUpdate ? "PUT" : "POST",
                contentType: "application/json",
                data: JSON.stringify(oData),
                success: (response) => {
                    if (response.success) {
                        MessageToast.show(response.message);
                        this.onClearForm();
                        this._loadEmployees();
                    }
                }
            });
        },

        // DELETE
        onDeleteEmployee: function () {
            const oData = this.getView().getModel("formModel").getData();
            
            MessageBox.confirm(`Remove ${oData.emp_name}?`, {
                onClose: (sAction) => {
                    if (sAction === MessageBox.Action.OK) {
                        $.ajax({
                            url: `${BASE_URL}/${oData.emp_id}`,
                            method: "DELETE",
                            success: (response) => {
                                MessageToast.show(response.message);
                                this.onClearForm();
                                this._loadEmployees();
                            }
                        });
                    }
                }
            });
        },

        // CLEAR
        onClearForm: function () {
            this.getView().getModel("formModel").setData({});
            this.byId("empTable").removeSelections(true);
        }
    });
});


Figure 4: Employee list loaded and profile form populated on row selection

 

Conclusion

In this blog post, we successfully built a complete CRUD application by connecting a SAPUI5 frontend to a Node.js and Express backend with a MySQL database. We covered how to fetch, create, update, and delete employee records using simple JSON data binding instead of OData services.

By completing this project, you now have a solid base for building full-stack business applications with open-source technologies.

Thank you for reading! Please leave a comment if you face any issues while setting up your project or running the CRUD operations.

entry.title: Enhancing Fiori Elements with Contact Quick View in RAP using CDS Annotation.
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

SAP Fiori Elements provides rich UI capabilities through annotations, reducing the need for custom UI development. One such feature is the Contact Quick View, which allows users to view contact information such as name, phone number, and address in a pop-up card directly from a table or object page.

In RAP applications, Contact Quick View can be implemented using CDS view annotations and UI metadata annotations. This blog shows how to create a contact entity and display the contact card in object page sections.

Why Contact Quick View?

A Contact Quick View typically act as a pop-up card that appears when a user clicks on a contact field in a Fiori application. It provides quick access to contact details without navigating to another screen.

Typical information displayed includes:

  • Full Name
  • Phone Number
  • Address
  • Email

This feature improves the user experience by making important contact information readily available.

STEP 1: Create a Contact CDS View.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Contact entity view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZI_CONTACT
{
  key id            as ID,

  @Semantics.name.fullName: true
  name              as Name,

  @Semantics.telephone.type: [#PREF]
  phone             as Phone,

  @Semantics.eMail.address: true
  email             as Email,

  @Semantics.address.label: true
  address_label     as AddressLabel
}

 

AnnotationPurpose
@Semantics.name.fullNameIdentifies the full name of the contact
@Semantics.telephone.typeDefines the preferred phone number
@Semantics.eMail.addressDefines the eMail address
@Semantics.address.labelAddress description

These semantic annotations enable SAP Fiori Elements to automatically build the Contact Quick View card.

STEP 2: Create an Association to the Contact Entity

In your root CDS view, create an association to the contact entity.

association [0..1] to ZI_CONTACT as _Contact 
on $projection.ContactID = _Contact.ID;

Expose the association:

_Conatct

STEP 3: Display Contact Quick View in Object Page.

To show the contact information in a Field Group or Header Section, use the #AS_CONTACT annotation.

@ui.facet: [ { purpose : #HEADER,
                 label : 'General Data',
                  type : #FIELDGROUP_REFERENCE,
       targetQualifier : 'HeaderData' } ]

@UI.fieldGroup: [ { qualifier : 'HeaderData',
                         type : #AS_CONTACT,
                        label : 'Contact Information',
                     position : 70,
                        value : '_Contact' } ]

When users open the Object Page, they will see a contact field. Clicking it opens the Contact Quick View card displaying all contact details.

To display the contact directly within a List Report table, use the following annotation:

@UI.lineItem: [
  {
    type      : #AS_CONTACT,
    label     : 'Contact',
    value     : '_Contact',
    position  : 120
  }
]

Output:

The Contact Quick View feature offers several advantages:

  • Improved user experience
  • Quick access to contact information
  • Reduced navigation effort
  • Standard SAP Fiori Elements functionality
  • Minimal custom UI development
  • Fully annotation-driven implementation

Key Points

  1. Contact data must be available through a dedicated CDS entity.
  2. Semantic annotations are mandatory for Fiori Elements to recognize contact information.
  3. The association to the contact entity must be exposed in the consumption view.
  4. Use type: #AS_CONTACT in @ui.lineItem for List Reports.
  5. Use type: #AS_CONTACT in @ui.fieldGroup for Object Pages.

Conclusion

Contact Quick View is a powerful Fiori Elements feature that enhances application usability by providing instant access to contact information. By leveraging CDS semantic annotations and the #AS_CONTACT UI annotation, RAP developers can implement this functionality with minimal effort and no custom UI coding.

Using this approach, SAP developers can deliver more interactive and user-friendly applications while fully embracing the annotation-driven development model of RAP and SAP Fiori Elements.

entry.title: How To Trigger Automated Email Notifications Based on KPI Thresholds in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

In many business scenarios, dashboards are expected to do more than just display KPIs. Business users also expect proactive alerts whenever critical numbers cross a certain threshold so that immediate action can be taken.

Recently, I worked on a requirement in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) where users needed to receive automatic notifications whenever a KPI exceeded a predefined limit. The alert had to be sent both as  an SAC notification and as an email. Another important requirement was that the process should run automatically on a specific day of the month without users having to open the dashboard manually.

In this blog, I’ll walk through the approach that was implemented using SAC scripting APIs such as getResultSet() and sendNotification() along with scheduled story publication.

Business Requirement

The requirement was straightforward but very useful from a business monitoring perspective.

The business wanted to:

  •             Monitor a KPI available in an SAC dashboard
  •             Compare the KPI value against a defined threshold
  •             Trigger an alert whenever the threshold condition is met
  •             Send email notifications to a predefined group of users
  •             Deliver alerts through: SAC notifications ,  Email notifications       
  •             Automate the entire process so that alerts are triggered on a scheduled date even if  nobody opens the dashboard

For example, if a KPI value becomes greater than 1000, users should automatically receive an alert.

Solution Overview

The solution was implemented using SAC Application Designer / Optimized Story scripting capabilities.

The main logic was written inside the On Initialization event of the story. Whenever the story initializes, the script reads the KPI value directly from the chart using the getResultSet() API.

The retrieved value is then converted into a numeric format and compared against the threshold value. If the condition is satisfied, the sendNotification() API triggers both SAC notifications and email alerts for the configured users.

To automate the process, the story publication was scheduled monthly to developers ID. During the scheduled execution, the story initializes automatically, which in turn executes the notification logic without requiring manual dashboard access.

High-Level Process Flow

The overall process works as follows:

  1.           The SAC story/dashboard initializes
  2.           The On Initialization script gets triggered
  3.           The script reads the KPI value from the chart using getResultSet()
  4.           The KPI value is converted into numeric format
  5.           The value is validated against the threshold
  6.           If the condition is met, a notification is prepared
  7.           SAC notification and email are sent to the configured users
  8.           Story publication scheduling automates the execution on the required date

During scheduled execution, the script runs automatically and sends alerts if needed.

Prerequisite

Before implementing this solution, ensure that email notifications are enabled in SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Technical Implementation

1. Fetching KPI Value from Chart

The KPI value can be accessed using the getResultSet() API available on charts and tables.

 Below is the logic used to fetch the KPI value:

 var check_data = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet()[0][Alias.MeasureDimension].rawValue;       This retrieves the raw KPI value from the chart result set.

2. Formatting the KPI Value

The returned value may sometimes be in string format, so it is important to convert it into a numeric value before comparison.

var value = Number.parseInt(check_data);

3. Threshold Validation

Once the value is converted, it can be validated using a simple conditional statement.

var notificationContent = ""; 
var title = ""; 
if(value > 1000){ 
    title = "Alert - The value is greater than 1000"; 
    notificationContent = "Dear User, Please be informed that the value is    greater than 1000"; 
} 
else{ 
    title = "Key figures look good";
    notificationContent = "Key figures look good"; 
}

4.  Sending Notifications

The sendNotification() API is used to send alerts to specific SAC users.

Application.sendNotification(
     {
        title: title,
        content: notificationContent,
        receivers: ["user_id"], 
        mode: ApplicationMode.Present,
        isSendEmail: true 
     });

This sends:

  •             An SAC notification
  •             An email notification (if email settings are enabled)

Complete Script

var check_data = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet()[0][Alias.MeasureDimension].rawValue;
 console.log(check_data); 
var value = Number.parseInt(check_data); 
var notificationContent = ""; 
var title = ""; 
if(value > 1000){
  title = "Alert - The value is greater than 1000"; 
  notificationContent = "Dear User, Please be informed that the value is greater than 1000"; 
} 
else{
  title = "Key figures look good";
  notificationContent = "Key figures look good"; 
}
 Application.sendNotification(
  { 
    title: title,
    content: notificationContent,
    receivers: ["user_id"],
    mode: ApplicationMode.Present,
    isSendEmail: true 
  });

Automating Alerts Using Scheduled Publication

Initially, notifications were getting triggered only when users opened the dashboard manually.

However, the requirement was to run the alerting process automatically on a specific day every month.

To achieve this, the SAC story publication feature was used.

The story was scheduled for publication to a specific user on the required date. During the scheduled execution, the story automatically initialized in the background, which triggered the On Initialization script.

As a result:

  •             KPI validation happened automatically
  •             Threshold conditions were checked
  •             Notifications and emails were sent automatically whenever the condition was met

This approach helped in building a simple but effective automated KPI monitoring mechanism inside SAP Analytics Cloud without requiring any external integration or custom scheduling tools.

OUTPUT EMAIL :

Final Thoughts

This solution is very useful for proactive business monitoring scenarios where users need immediate visibility into KPI fluctuations.

Using SAC scripting APIs along with scheduled publication makes it possible to build lightweight alerting mechanisms directly within SAP Analytics Cloud.

Some additional enhancements that can be explored:

  •             Dynamic threshold configuration
  •             Multiple KPI monitoring
  •             Role-based recipient management
  •             Integration with external notification channels
  •             Exception dashboards for alert tracking

I hope this blog helps anyone looking to implement automated KPI-based alerts in SAC.

If you are here, thank you so much for taking out your time to read this. Your feedback will be highly appreciated.

Thanks,
Himanshu

entry.title: Implementing Parallel Processing in ABAP Cloud Using CL_ABAP_PARALLEL and RAP EML
entry.content:
Introduction:


Modern SAP applications often need to execute time-consuming operations such as external service calls, heavy calculations, or mass data creation. Running such logic sequentially can slow down user interaction and block dialog processes. ABAP Cloud offers a clean and powerful way to distribute workload using parallel processing via the class CL_ABAP_PARALLEL.

In this blog, we will see:

  1. What a parallel class is in ABAP Cloud
  2. How to implement IF_ABAP_PARALLEL
  3. How to execute RAP EML inside a parallel task
  4. How to commit inside the task and return results

What is parallel class?


A parallel class is a normal ABAP class that implements the interface IF_ABAP_PARALLEL.
This interface provides the method: IF_ABAP_PARALLEL-DO

which represents the logic that will run in a separate work process. Each instance of this class becomes an independent task executed by the framework.

The advantages are:

  • Multiple tasks run concurrently
  • Main session is not blocked
  • Each task has its own transactional context
  • You can perform COMMIT inside the task

Business scenario:

  • Billing Document Header, which represents the main business document
  • Billing Document Item, which contains detailed line-level data including materials
  • Task BO (a separate RAP Business Object with managed implementation) used to store task-related information.

Requirement:

  • Read the billing document item data from the system using RAP EML
  • Fetch the corresponding Material ID from each billing item for further processing
  • Create a Task record using RAP EML based on the extracted Material
  • Perform the commit operation inside the parallel task to ensure independent transaction handling
  • Retrieve the generated Task UUID from the managed Task BO after successful creation
  • Update the billing item description field with the returned UUID to reflect the processing result
  • Ensure all these steps are executed efficiently using parallel processing for better performance and scalability.

Implementing Steps:

Step 1: Create parallel task class
This class:

  • Receives Material ID in constructor
  • Implements IF_ABAP_PARALLEL
  • Creates Task using EML
  • Performs COMMIT ENTITIES inside the task
  • Returns the created UUID
CLASS zsrt_cl_parallel_task DEFINITION
PUBLIC
FINAL
CREATE PUBLIC .

PUBLIC SECTION.
INTERFACES if_serializable_object . " Required for parallel execution
INTERFACES if_abap_parallel . " Enables parallel processing logic

METHODS :
constructor IMPORTING iv_material TYPE string, " Receive input material
get_result RETURNING VALUE(rt_result) TYPE string. " Return generated UUID

PRIVATE SECTION.
DATA: mv_material TYPE string. " Stores material passed to task
DATA mv_result TYPE string. " Stores generated Task UUID

ENDCLASS.



CLASS zsrt_cl_parallel_task IMPLEMENTATION.

METHOD constructor.
mv_material = iv_material. " Initialize material for this task instance
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD get_result.
RETURN mv_result. " Return UUID result to caller
ENDMETHOD.


METHOD if_abap_parallel~do.

DATA:
lt_task_create TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE zsrt_i_task_head, " Input structure for CREATE
ls_task_create LIKE LINE OF lt_task_create,
lt_reported TYPE RESPONSE FOR REPORTED EARLY zsrt_i_task_head,
lt_mapped TYPE RESPONSE FOR MAPPED EARLY zsrt_i_task_head, " Holds generated values (UUID)
lt_failed TYPE RESPONSE FOR FAILED EARLY zsrt_i_task_head. " Holds failed entries

" Prepare Task data using material
lt_task_create = VALUE #(
(
%cid = 'MY_CID_1' " Correlation ID
taskid = |TASK-{ mv_material }| " Dynamic Task ID
title = |New task for { mv_material }| " Task title
description = 'Test Description'
overallstatus = 'O'
%control = VALUE #( " Mark fields for update
taskid = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
title = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
description = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
overallstatus = if_abap_behv=>mk-on )
)
).

" Create Task record using RAP EML
MODIFY ENTITIES OF zsrt_i_task_head
ENTITY zsrt_i_task_head
CREATE FROM lt_task_create
MAPPED lt_mapped " Contains generated UUID
FAILED lt_failed
REPORTED lt_reported.

" Process only if creation is successful
IF lt_failed IS INITIAL.

COMMIT ENTITIES. " Commit inside parallel task (separate LUW)

" Read generated UUID from mapped structure
READ TABLE lt_mapped-zsrt_i_task_head INTO DATA(ls_map) INDEX 1.

IF sy-subrc = 0.
mv_result = ls_map-taskuuid. " Store UUID for returning
ENDIF.

ENDIF.

ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.


Key Points:

  • EML is executed inside the parallel session
  • COMMIT ENTITIES is allowed here
  • Result is stored in instance attribute
  • get_result method exposes the value

Step 2: Calling the Parallel Class
To keep the example simple, we call it from a local class implementing IF_OO_ADT_CLASSRUN.

Execution Flow

  • Read billing item using EML
  • Extract Material ID
  • Create instance of parallel task
  • Run using CL_ABAP_PARALLEL
  • Collect result
  • Update billing item description
CLASS zsrt_cl_process DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.   " Entry point for execution in ADT

ENDCLASS.



CLASS zsrt_cl_process IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.

    DATA: 
      lt_processes TYPE cl_abap_parallel=>t_in_inst_tab,  " Holds parallel task instances
      lt_results   TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF sysuuid_x16.    " Stores returned UUID results

    " Read all Billing Items from DB table
    SELECT *
      FROM zsrt_bill_item
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_items).


    " Create one parallel task instance per item
    LOOP AT lt_items INTO DATA(ls_item).

      INSERT NEW zsrt_cl_parallel_task(
        CONV #( ls_item-materialid )   " Pass material to parallel class
      ) INTO TABLE lt_processes.

    ENDLOOP.


    " Execute all tasks in parallel (max 5 at a time)
    NEW cl_abap_parallel( p_num_tasks = 5 )->run_inst(
      EXPORTING
        p_in_tab  = lt_processes       " Input: task instances
      IMPORTING
        p_out_tab = DATA(lt_finished)  " Output: completed instances
    ).


    " Collect UUID results from each parallel execution
    LOOP AT lt_finished INTO DATA(ls_finished).

      DATA(lv_uuid) =
        CAST zsrt_cl_parallel_task(
          ls_finished-inst )->get_result( ).  " Get UUID from each task

      APPEND lv_uuid TO lt_results.

    ENDLOOP.


    DATA idx TYPE sy-tabix.
    idx = 0.


    " Update billing items with respective UUIDs
    LOOP AT lt_items INTO ls_item.

      idx = idx + 1.

      READ TABLE lt_results INTO DATA(lv_uuid2) INDEX idx.

      IF sy-subrc = 0.

        MODIFY ENTITIES OF zsrt_i_bill_head
          ENTITY zsrt_i_bill_item
          UPDATE FROM VALUE #(
            ( billid               = ls_item-billid       " Key field
              itemno               = ls_item-itemno       " Key field
              description          = lv_uuid2             " Store UUID
              %control-description = if_abap_behv=>mk-on  " Enable update
            )
          ).

      ENDIF.

    ENDLOOP.


    " Save all updates to database
    COMMIT ENTITIES.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

Result:


Conclusion:
Parallel processing in ABAP Cloud enables faster and efficient handling of large data by executing tasks simultaneously. Using CL_ABAP_PARALLEL with RAP EML, we can process multiple records independently and improve performance. This approach ensures better scalability and optimal use of system resources for real-time business scenarios.

entry.title: Multiple Risk Owners Generated for IBP Cloud Risks in GRC AC when we have IAG as Bridge
entry.content:
We have integrated SAP IBP (Cloud) with SAP IAG, and IAG is being used as a bridge between IBP and SAP GRC Access Control (AC).

For each IBP risk, we have assigned the P-user ID that belongs to the IAG_WF_RISKOWNER user group. Since risk approvals and mitigations are performed in GRC AC, we have maintained the approver mapping in IAG by mapping the RISKOWNER to the corresponding AC Risk Owner.

However, when cloud risks from IBP are brought into GRC AC, we are seeing multiple risk owners assigned to the same risk instead of a single risk owner (or a limited set of risk owners).

We also observe that the GRACSODRISKOWN table contains multiple risk owner entries for the same cloud risk. As part of the setup, we have:

  • Performed a repository synchronisation for the IBP connector.
  • Enabled the IAG Role Import option.
  • Completed all synchronisation activities and configuration steps mentioned in the SAP standard documentation.

Our question is:

How can we restrict or control the risk owner assignment so that an IBP cloud risk is mapped to only one (or a limited number of) AC risk owner(s), instead of multiple risk owners being created in GRC AC?

Could you please advise if there is any configuration, synchronisation setting, or best practice that can help achieve this?

entry.title: SII Tip for Spain #13: Pro-rata
entry.content:
Welcome to another SII Tip for Spain. The pro-rata (prorrata de IVA) is one of those topics that looks straightforward on paper but hides surprises when SII gets involved. In this post I'll walk you through how pro-rata and SII interact in S/4HANA Public Cloud and Private Cloud, what can go wrong if the configuration is incomplete, and, most importantly, how to fix it.

 

Business Scenario

VAT pro-rata (prorrata del IVA) is defined in LIVA Article 102 and applies to businesses that make deliveries of goods or services, some of them taxable and others VAT-exempt. The typical example is leasing activities: the rental of residential properties is a VAT-exempt transaction with no right to deduct input tax, whereas the rental of commercial premises is a taxable and non-exempt transaction with the right to deduct input tax.

These companies are not allowed to deduct 100% of their input VAT. Instead, they may choose to apply a general or a special pro-rata algorithm. Further details about these algorithms are available in the Tax Agency portal (“Pro rata rule”).

In the simplest case (general), the pro-rata coefficient is the ratio [only turnovers with deductible VAT] / [ total turnover, deductible + not deductible], rounded up to the nearest whole number. This pro-rata percentage is calculated with the information of the year Y1, and will be used as provisional during Y2. At the end of Y2, a recalculation of the definitive pro-rata will take place, and the corresponding adjustments shall be reported in the corresponding VAT return (legal form 303).

For example, a company is dedicated to renting homes and business premises. The operations carried out during year Y1 were the following:

  • Rental of housing: 12,000 euros (input VAT is NOT deductible)
  • Rental of business premises: 32,000 euros (input VAT is deductible)

The “temporary” pro-rata for Y2 will be 32000€/42000€ = 72.72727% => 73%. IMPORTANT: The pro-rata percentage determination has to be carried out outside SAP.

The input VAT supported in year Y2 by expenses used in real estate (both homes and premises) is: Tax base = 6500€, input VAT = 1365€. In this case:

  • Deductible Input VAT = 1365€*73% = 996,45€
  • NON-Deductible Input VAT = 1365€*27% = 368,55€

At the end of Y2, the definitive pro-rata is calculated with the real turnovers of the year. Provided that the definitive pro-rata is 70%, the VAT deductions have to be recalculated. In this case, 1365€*70% = 955,5€. Since there is an excess of deduction of 996,45€-955,5€=40,95€, this this year-end adjustment has to be reported in the legal form 303 in Q4 (but not in SII).

 

Customizing hints in S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition

In Public Cloud, the pro-rata split is applied automatically when it finds a coefficient entry in the "Define Pro Rata Coefficients" activity (SSCUI 103619) for the tax code. 

In S/4HANA public Cloud Edition, the pro-rata management is described in Pro Rata VAT Processing

1) Define the tax code (FTXP / SSCUI 101016) for country ES:

    • MWVS → VST, 21% (deductible)
    • MWVZ → NVV, 0% placeholder (non-deductible)

2) Define the pro-rata coefficient in SSCUI 103619:

NOTE:  If the tax code is not configured in this table and it defines the NVV Account Key, this value is ignored and the full VAT amount will be posted under the account key VST as  100% deductible (and then CuotaDeducible equals the full CuotaSoportada in SII). When creating the incoming invoice, the system warns at posting time with message RAT007 "Pro rata VAT: Coefficient not defined or out of range (0%-100%)" but does not block the document posting.

3) Assign SII attributes in SSCUI 102558

 

4) Define Final Pro Rata Coefficients for Year-End Adjustment (SSCUI 105523)

 

 

Customizing hints in S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition / ECC

In S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition and in ECC systems, the pro-rata is described in SAP note 801318 - Prorata VAT Solution and in the help portal VAT Pro-Rata Adjustment. The note 801318 is a consulting solution that requires development, so you may need to contact your implementation partner.

As a high level vision of this note:

1) Tax procedure. Copy the standard Spain procedure, add a new condition(step 105, base value routine 907), assign calculation routine 935 to both MWVS (step 120) and MWVZ (step 150).

2) ABAP routines. In a high level view, the deductible/non deductible split is calculated at posting time by the ABAP calculation routine 907 and Routine 935.

3) Condition records  per company code with the annual pro-rata percentage. This is important to calculate the right non-deductible amount.

4) Pro-Rata Validation Entity Values. For the routine 935 to fire, the tax code must be registered in the Pro-Rata Validation Entity values.

NOTE: if the pro-rata realated tax code is not configured properly in this step and/or  the ABAP routines 907 or 935 are incorrectly implemented (e.g., wrong basis value), the deductible and non-deductible rates can fail to add up to a recognized VAT amount by the Tax agency (or be reported as 0%). The Tax Agench SII may issue the error code 1166: "Valor del campo TipoImpositivo no esta incluido en la lista de valores permitidos"  (The value of TipoImpositivo is not a the valid value).

5) Assign SII attributes to the pro-rata tax code (IMG equivalent of SSCUI 102558).

6) Assign the definitive pro-rata at year-end. You can use Transaction Code S_RFIDPTDPR “Definitive Pro-Rata coefficient”  to define the definitive pro-rata percentage and any other periodic (monthly) coefficients if needed:

 

 

 

Year-End Adjustments processes

Once the definitive pro-rata is calculated in Q4, it is necessary to adjust the excess/defect of deductions reported based on the “temporary” pro-rata coefficient during the year. These adjustments will be reported in the legal form 303 but not in the SII register books for incoming invoices.

In S/4HANA Public cloud edition,  use the Schedule Tax Jobs Fiori app → Year-End Adjustment of Pro Rata VAT to calculate the adjustment postings.

In S/4PHANA private cloud edition and ECC, you can use transaction S_RFID_PTVPRADPRC00 “Pro-Rata adjustments – Calculation” to calculate the adjustment postings based on the definitive pro-rata

The resulting adjustment postings will be similar to this one:

 

 

SII process

For example an incoming invoice of 1000€ Tax base, 21% VAT, pro-rata coefficient = 50%, is expected to be posted as:

 

Debit

Debit

Debit

Vendor payable

 

1,210 €

Expense

1,000 €

 

Non-deductible VAT (NVV , absorbed in cost)

105 €

 

Input VAT (VST, deductible)

105 €

 

When correctly customized as described, the Financial document for this example should be similar to this one:

The XML reported to SII should be similar to the following:

 

 

Further Reading

In case of additional questions, you can visit the SII help documentation in the following links:

  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in ERP
  • Other SII tips: Spain - SAP Document and Reporting Compliance → Related Links → Community section

Disclaimer

This blog post reflects the author's interpretation of Spanish legal requirements and SAP system behavior as of the publication date. It is intended for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice or consulting guide. For specific guidance, consult your legal or tax advisor and refer to official SAP documentation.

The links to external sources in this blog may be modified at any time externally by the owner without previous notice.
